exemplar.title:
An experiment to investigate the effects of exercise
on memory recall [a replication of Labban & Etnier (2011])

detailedExemplarData.title:
An experiment to investigate the effects of exercise
on memory recall [a replication of Labban & Etnier (2011])

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and well-articulated aim linked to both practical study strategies and theoretical relevance
Comprehensive description of the YerkesâDodson Law with neurobiological detail and explicit connection to IV/DV
Precise operationalisation of IV and DV in the hypothesis statement
weaknesses: Minor brevity in explaining broader societal value
Reliance on secondary websites rather than primary literature for some citations
Hypothesis section could integrate terminology exactly as used in operational definitions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets the criteria for band 5: the aim is clearly stated and its relevance explained, the theory/model is described with an explicit link to the investigation, and the hypothesis and variables are correctly operationalised. Minor brevity in discussing wider societal impact and use of secondary sources prevent assigning the absolute top mark of 6, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but explained in terms of validity (practice effects)
Opportunity sampling technique is justified with demographic detail and rationale
Controlled variables and choice of materials are described and explicitly justified for internal validity
weaknesses: No mention of blinding to mitigate expectancy bias
Opportunity sampling critique could address mitigation of selection bias
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three exploration strands fall solidly in the top band (3â4): the design is explained rather than merely described, sampling technique and participant choice are reasoned, and controlled variables/materials are justified. There are minor omissions (e.g. blinding), but these do not drop the work below the bandâs standard, warranting the full score of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of median and IQR with clear justification for non-parametric data
Correct application of MannâWhitney U test with accurate p-value reporting
Graph is well labelled, addresses the hypothesis, and findings are explicitly interpreted in relation to the data and hypothesis
weaknesses: Inconsistency in reported sample size (n = 26 vs 27)
Graph lacks error-dispersion bars (IQR or variance)
Language overstates causal inference given nonrandomised allocation and omits effect size
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates appropriate and accurate application of both descriptive and inferential statistics, a correctly presented graph, and a thorough interpretation linked to the hypothesis. Minor reporting inconsistencies and missing supplementary details (error bars, effect size) prevent the full 6, placing the work in band 5 and justifying a score of 5.

strengths: Findings are discussed with clear reference to both the original study and the YDL framework
Strengths and limitations are relevant and described in light of validity concerns
Proposed modifications are well justified and explicitly linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion stops short of deeper critical comparison (e.g. varied exercise intensities, delay lengths)
Some suggested sampling modifications (volunteer sampling) may introduce self-selection bias
Concluding reflections on generalizability and ethical/future research considerations are limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation contains strong discussion and well-justified modifications, placing it in band 3â4 overall. While strengths/limitations and modifications are relevant and explained, the level of critical depth and broader reflection falls just short of the 5â6 band, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar chart is an appropriate technique for displaying two independent medians, is fully labelled, includes the numerical values, and visually addresses the hypothesis.  Axis scaling is sensible.  There are no major errors, though inclusion of dispersion bars would further strengthen it; thus 5/6 is justified.

The student explicitly states the statistical outcome, rejects the null, links the significance to a âcause-and-effect relationship,â and clearly ties this back to the operationalised hypotheses.  Interpretation is thorough and error-free, meriting full marks in the top band.

Both null and research hypotheses are present.  The IV is operationalised precisely as âthree sets of 15 jumping jacks within 1.5 minutes,â and the DV is operationalised as ânumber of words correctly recalled from a 20-word list.â  This meets the top-band criteria fully and without error, so the maximum score in the band is justified.

Opportunity sampling is defined, the demographic characteristics are described, and the rationale (convenience, representativeness of advisories) is offered.  This constitutes an explanation of both technique and choice, satisfying the higher band; minor over-reliance on convenience without deeper critique justifies a 4 rather than full marks.

The Yerkes-Dodson Law is accurately described, expanded with neurobiological detail (adrenaline, Î±2B-adrenoceptors, amygdalaâhippocampus pathways), and explicitly connected to how acute exercise should modulate long-term memory recall.  The link between theory and the specific IV/DV is made explicit (âexercise â arousal â enhanced recallâ), fulfilling the requirement for an explained connection.  A couple of citations are secondary websites rather than primary literature, so a marginal deduction within the band is warranted, giving 5 rather than 6.

Findings are discussed in relation to both the replicated Labban & Etnier study and the YDL, demonstrating understanding of theoretical implications.  The discussion, while coherent, stops short of deeper critical comparison (e.g., contrasting intensity, delay length) that would warrant a 6, so a solid 5 is appropriate.

The student clearly states the aim â to determine whether completing three sets of 15 jumping jacks before exposure to a 20-word list affects the number of words recalled by Grade 11 students â and then explicitly explains why this is relevant (offering an easy strategy for students to enhance academic performance).  The relevance is linked to both practical (study techniques) and theoretical (testing YDL) considerations, satisfying the descriptor for a full explanation.  Minor brevity in discussing broader societal value keeps the response from being exemplary but still fits comfortably in the top band.

Multiple strengths and limitations (design, sampling, materials) are each explained and explicitly linked to validity concerns, meeting the descriptor for âexplained and relevant.â  The breadth is good, but some points (e.g., acquiescence bias) could be evidenced with data, so 5/6 is awarded.

Controlled variables (standardised instructions, identical timing, identical word list) are named and each is explicitly justified in terms of reducing bias or increasing validity.  The choice of materials (jumping jacks, generic 4â7-letter words) is also explained.  This level of justification meets the top of the 3-4 band.

For each stated limitation the student proposes a concrete, well-justified modification (matched-pairs for participant variability, volunteer sampling for acquiescence bias, standardised video for exercise form).  The causal link to improved validity is clearly articulated, satisfying âexplicitly linked and fully justified,â so full marks are appropriate.

The student not only states that an independent-measures design was used but also explains the choice (avoidance of practice effects, time efficiency).  This moves beyond mere description into explanation, matching the 3-4 band; the explanation is clear and directly connected to validity concerns, so the top of the band (4) is appropriate.

Appropriate descriptive (median, IQR) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are selected for non-parametric, independent-groups, small-sample data.  Calculations appear correct (U = 22.5 < critical 54, p =.00066).  Minor inconsistencies in reported N (26 vs 27) prevent the award of the absolute maximum, resulting in 5.

annotations.comment: The student provides a comprehensive description of the YerkesâDodson Law, integrating neurobiological mechanisms and clearly linking increased arousal to memory consolidation in the hippocampus. This establishes a strong theoretical foundation for the study.

The summary of Labban & Etnier (2011) accurately captures the experimental conditions, but the description of the dependent variable could be refined by specifying paragraph elements operationally and including parameters such as delay duration for clarity.
The choice of Grade 11 students is explained for maturity and relevance, but the potential for sample bias remains; consider discussing mitigation strategies for opportunistic selection.
The independent-measures design is explained with respect to practice effects, highlighting validity considerations and appropriate allocation procedures.
The procedural description covers ethical considerations and debriefing comprehensively, ensuring participant rights and confidentiality, which strengthens procedural validity.
Controlled variables are extensively detailed, including standardised scripts, timing, and materials, demonstrating rigorous internal validity control.

While flashbulb memory is invoked to support the YDL, the connection between flashbulb memory mechanisms and this specific wordâlist recall task could be more explicit to strengthen theoretical relevance.

The description of modifications to the original study effectively justifies methodological changes, but this section should more explicitly state how these changes test the same theoretical predictions of the YDL.
The aim is clearly articulated and linked to Grade 11 academic performance strategies, demonstrating both relevance and context-specific applicability.
The null hypothesis correctly identifies the IV and DV but omits operational definitions; consider specifying the exact exercise protocol and recall metric in the hypothesis statement.
The research hypothesis specifies directionality and variables accurately; ensure consistency by matching terminology used in the operational definitions (e.g. âthree sets of 15 jumping jacksâ).
The opportunity sampling method is well described with demographic details and rationale, demonstrating clear sampling design and context-specific justification.
Although procedures are standardised, the absence of participant or researcher blinding may introduce expectancy biases; consider implementing a double-blind protocol.
Appropriate use of median and IQR for skewed data is well justified, reflecting sound statistical reasoning for measure selection.
The descriptive table is clear and correctly labelled, but adding units (e.g. âwords recalledâ) in the header would enhance interpretability.
The column chart addresses the hypothesis effectively, though inclusion of error bars (e.g. IQR whiskers) would provide a fuller visual representation of dispersion.
The student accurately interprets differences in median and IQR between conditions, linking central tendency and dispersion to group performance.
There is an inconsistency in sample size (n = 26), whereas 27 participants were stated earlier; ensure consistency in reporting to avoid confusion in the analysis.

The MannâWhitney U Test is applied correctly with appropriate critical value comparison, but the language âcause-and-effectâ is overstated given nonrandomized group allocation; use more cautious phrasing.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g. \(r = Z/\sqrt{N}\)) to complement p-values and provide information on the magnitude of differences.
Findings are thoughtfully discussed in relation to both the original study and the YDL, demonstrating effective linking of results to theoretical frameworks.
The proposed matched-pairs modification is well justified by the identified limitation (participant variability), showing a clear link between design improvement and identified confound.
Opportunity sampling strengths are clearly articulated with respect to convenience and representativeness, reflecting balanced evaluation of sampling technique.
The limitation of acquiescence bias is relevant, but volunteer sampling may introduce self-selection bias; consider discussing additional sampling strategies to mitigate both issues.
Procedure and materials strengths are well identified through elimination of word-related biases, strengthening internal validity.
The study conclusion reiterates overall findings but could be nuanced by addressing generalizability limits, such as applicability to different age groups or contexts.
The procedural limitation regarding operationalisation of jumping jacks is well identified; suggesting a standardized video is a practical modification that directly addresses this issue.
The final summary acknowledges broader contributions but should integrate a brief critical reflection on ethical considerations or potential future research directions.
